California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
April 1,5, 199l-
Neil H. Mccabe
Chief Deputy county counsel
county of Butte
25 county center Drive
oroviIle, CA 95965-3380
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. I -91-09 3
Dear Mr . l,lccabe :
You are seeking advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the potiticat Reform Act (the naqtrr1.1/ The county
of Butte is in the process of updating its conflict of interest
code and is questioning the need to include certain positions.
OUESTION
Should the conflict of interest code for the Butte county
District Attorney's Office designate the chief deputy district
attorney-civil; 
chief dePuty district 
attorney-crirnina 1 ,' and the
chief investigator/administrative assistant?
CONCLUS ION
Based on a review of the iob descriptl-ons provided, it
appears that those positions are the type which the Butte County
eoird of Supervisors should consider including in the code for the
district 
attorney's office.
l/ 
covernment Code Sections 81ooo-91015. AJ-I statutory
references are to the Government code unLess otherwise indicated.
commission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18000; et seq. all references to regu)-ations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428J Street, Suite 8Oo o P.O. Box 8o7 o Sacrarnento CA 958o4-o8o7 . (916)322'5660


FACTS
The county of Butte is updating its conflict of interest
code. I,lithin the office of the district 
attorney, the proposed
new code designates the chief deputy district 
attorney-civi1,
chief deputy district 
attorney-criminal , and the chief
invest igitoiT "dmini"trative assistant with the broadest disclosure
possible.
The district 
attorney and the individuals in these positions
are questioning the need ior these positions to be included in the
code.- It is tieir contention that any decision rnade regarding the
expenditure of $3oo or more for gToods or services is made only by
th; district 
attorney. They state that independent ethical
considerations would require them to disqualify themselves or be
recused from any case involving any personaf interest, and further
have expressed Loncern that disclosure of personal inforrnation
could nike them vulnerable to menbers of the criminal population
with which they deal.
You provided us with copies of job descriptions for the three
positionsandhaveaskediftheyareofthetypewhichshouldbe
io.r"rea by the conflict of interest code. The job descriptions
for the dlputy district 
attorneys contain simil-ar language and
provide in-pait that the positions rr. -.review and examine
Lvidence, i-nterrogate witnesses, determine lrhether or not a crirne
has been comrnitted. . . . . and whether or not the evidence justifies
prosecution." The job descriptions further provide that the
positions r...nake Lentative decisions concerning advisability to
irosecute, cornprornise, or disrniss criminal litigation 
and discuss
these problems with superiors. "z/
The chief investigator / administrator' s job description sets
out different duties. It provides in part that this person
"...consults with the district 
attorney in the formulation of
major administrative policies; acts for the District Attorney in
adirinistrative matters. . . eva luates staff and equipnent
needs. . . reviews / approves expenditures; sj-gns purchase orders/
requisitions...diilcts 
the preparation of cases for prosecution;
appears / provides testimony in court.rl
Page Two
April ■5, ■99■
2l 
These excerpts are frorn job descriptions frorn the deputy
district 
attorney classification. 
The same or sinil-ar
descriptions are also part of the job descriptions for chief
deputy positions. 
Therefore, the fotlowing analysis may not apply
so1eIy to the chief deputy positions which are the subject of this
letter.


Page Three
April 15, 1991
ANALYS I S
Government Code Sectior. a73o2 requires that a conflict of
interest code enumerate the positions within an allency, other than
those specified in Section A72Oo, which involve the making or
participation in the rnakinq of decisions whj'ch may have a
@ria1 
financial effect on any economic
interest and for each such enumerated positions, the specific
types of investrnents, business positions, interests in real
prtperty, and sources of income which are reportable'
The responsibility for deterrnining if a code meets the
specif icatio-ns of the act rests with the rrcode reviewing bodyrr
,t", 
i" this instance, is the Butte county. Board of Supervisors'
is"iti"" 
87303.) For this reason the Comrnission can only provide
inforrnal assistance. 3/
Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) provides some guj'dance in
identifying designated positions:
(b) A public official 
'tmakes a sqvernnental
decision,rr... when he or she, acting within the
autnority of his or her office:
(1) votes on a matter;
(2) APPoints a Person;
(3) obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action;
(4) Enters into anY contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her
agency; or
(5) Deternines not to act, within the
rneaning of subparagraphs (1) through
(4)...
(c) A public official 
or designated empLoyee 
-
"partici,pates in the makinq of .a qovernmental decision'l
wnen, a"ting within the authority of his or her
position, he or she:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental
entity or private person regarding the
decision,' or
)t 
rnforma] assistance does not
imnunity provided bY an oPinion
(Governnent Code Section 83114;
1832e (c) (3) . )
provide the requestor with the
or fornal written advice.
2 Cal. code of Regs. Section


Page Four
April 
■5′  ■99■
(2) Advises or makes reconmendations to the
decisionrnaker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive
review, bY:
(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise
of judgment on the part of the official
or designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
- 
analysis or oPinion, orally or in
writi.ngT, which requires the exercise of
j udgrrnent on the part of the official 
or
designated emPloyee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision.
Regulation 18700 (b) and (c),
emPhasis added.
It is foreseeable that an individual- who is the subject of a
crirninal investigation could be a source of income to a deputy
district 
attorney. For example, the individuaL could be a source-
of income througl a landlord-tenant relationship or could be a
business partnei to the deputy or to.his or her spouse' If'
;;;r;;;; 
is the subject of an investigation, the same or similar
concerns arise. 
The deputy or his or her spouse could have an
investment in the businLss-under investigation or the business
under j-nvestigation could be a cornpetitor to the deputy's
business.
The deputy district 
attorneys, in their day-to-day
activities,- r"irie, evidence and arrest records and determine
whether or not a crime has been comrnitted which warrants
prosecution. 
The deputies are also making decj-sions on the
iroposed resol,ution oi cases. They are not only making that
iniliar 
determination of whether or not to prosecute but are
active in negotiating the resolution of the case.
The end result of their deternination could affect the
j.ncome-producing potential of an individual, should that
individual be inclrcerated, or affect the prosPerity of a business
if the business is the subject of a civil or crininal action.
Should any individual- or entity which is an econonic interest
of the deputy be the subject of an j-nvestigation, the deputy will
have to dlsqualify himself or herself fron participating in aII
aspects of the case from the initial 
deterrnination through
reiolution of the action i-f it is foreseeabfe that the decision
wilI have a material financial effect on the individual or entity.
(section 87100.) In this case, aly financial effect would be
considered material. 
(Regulation 18702.1, copy enclosed. )


Page Five
ApriI 15, 19 91
Therefore based on the inforrnation provided, it appears that
the deputy district 
attorneys make or participate in the making of
decisions which nay foreseeably have a material financial- effect
on their economic interests (Section 87302), and the board of
supervisors should consider including thern in the conflict of
interest code for Butte CountY.
The chief investigator/adninistrative 
assistant appears to be
the person who formulaies major policy on behalf of the district
attoiney. 
The job description indicates he or she I'evaluates
equipment needsr r' "approves expendituresrr and further rrdirects the
piepiration of cases ind provides testimony in court." 
This
po=ition could not only affect individuals and businesses which
lre the subject of an investigation but could also affect
interests which provide services, supplies or equiprnent of-the
type utilized by the district 
attorney's office. 
This position
allo should be considered for inclusion in the code.
In your letter you indicated that the deputy district
attorney-s believe th-y should not be covered by the Act silce
indepenient ethical considerations require then to disqualify
themsel-ves.
section 81013 provides that nothing shall Prevent a l-ocal
agency from imposi,ng additional requirements on any person if the
r6quiiements do not prevent the person from complyj-ng with the
poiitical 
Reform Act-. While theie may be other lahrs or provisions
which govern the conduct of the deputy district 
attorneys, those
other laws or provisions are in addition to, and do not supersede,
the requirernents of the poLitical Reform Act. The deputy district
attorneys are sti11 subject to the provisions of the Act.
They further contend that disclosure of personal interests
make them vulnerable to the crininal element.
There is nothing in the Act which grants relief to public
officials 
who believe disclosure of their financial interests may
subject then to harassnent. The Act's disclosure provisions apply
to judges who hear and sentence criminal cases as v'e1l as to the
Cov6rn6r and members of the Legislature. 
The constitutiona l ity of
the Act's discfosure provisions has already been considered and
upheld. 
The right of privacy does not prevail over the-right of
t'he public to an honest and inpartial qovernment, as implemented
by ILgislation requirinq a variety of public officials 
and
candidates to disCl-ose their financial interests. 
See Countv of
Nevada v MacMill,an (1974) 11 Cat. 3d 662 and Havs v wood (1979) 25
cal. 3 772; L6O Cal. Rptr. 102, 603 P.2d l-9.


Page Six
Apr.i1 15, 1991
I hope this
anything further
322-5904.
sH/JET/ jt
Enc l osures
Sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
infonnation has assisted you. If there is
you need, pl,ease feel free to contact me at (916)
PoliticaI Reform Consultant
Legal Division
